actionable_feedback: Produce a small CSV export (cbtc_braking_margin_cells.csv) that contains the key formula cells and their values (one row per train with columns: speed_kmh, v_mps, latency_s, emergency_mps2, stopping_distance_m, separation_m, available_margin_m, classification). This allows validators without openpyxl to inspect exact cell outputs.
Include a screenshot (PNG) of the workbook showing the formula bar and the key rows (or an exported PDF) so reviewers can see that formulas are embedded rather than only reported in prose.

Run the workbook-parsing/check step in an environment with openpyxl available and attach the parser-run log or a short verification artifact (workbook_parsed_ok.txt) that confirms cell-level parsing succeeded.

Document the numeric rounding / precision policy in safety_case_addendum.md (e.g., number of decimal places displayed, rounding mode used) and add a short table showing exact recomputed values to at least 3-6 decimal places so any small differences are transparent.

Add a short justification for the classification thresholds (why warn is 35-45 m and why pass >45 m), referencing the safety or operational rationale or pointing to organizational policy if applicable.
In the addendum, explicitly note that service_brake_mps2 is present in the packet but intentionally not used in the stopping-distance formula, and why (keeps traceability and avoids confusion).

Include reproducible sensitivity seeds/procedure: provide exact delta values used (e.g., +/-5 km/h) and the exact formulas/cell references so others can rerun the sensitivity checks deterministically. Optionally provide a small script (recompute_margins.py) that reads input/task.json and reproduces the workbook calculations, asserting differences within a defined tolerance.

If possible, attach a one-page QA checklist (qa_checks.txt) showing the smoke-check flags and that visual render verification was attempted (with note about LibreOffice absence) so future runs confirm rendering availability or document how to render screenshots locally.
artifact_specific_comments: safety_case_addendum.md: Good explicit statement of formulas, per-train intermediate values, and embedded packet citations. Addendum should include a short rounding/precision policy and threshold justification.

cbtc_braking_margin.xlsx: Workbook_formula_smoke_check=true indicates embedded formulas exist, but verifier could not parse the file in this environment. Provide CSV/screenshot and re-run parsing in an env with openpyxl.
feedback_summary: Baseline deliverables are present and semantically consistent with the stopping-distance formula; overall decision = pass. Two minor verifier warnings: (1) the .xlsx could not be parsed in the verifier environment (openpyxl unavailable), so semantic verification relied on the addendum text and workbook smoke-check flag; (2) small numeric rounding differences (<=0.4 m) were observed between recomputed raw values and the addendum. Recommend adding explicit artifacts and documentation to remove parser uncertainty and to state rounding/precision policy.

acceptance_criteria_for_revision: CSV and screenshot are attached and referenced by the addendum.
Parser log shows successful cell-level parsing.
Rounding/precision policy is documented in the addendum.
Recompute script shows differences <= stated tolerance (e.g., 0.5 m).
Verifier warnings about .xlsx parsing and rounding are resolved.
verification_steps_after_revision: Confirm openpyxl-based parsing succeeds and attach parser log.
Run recompute_margins.py and confirm all assertions pass; attach run log.
Re-run verifier and ensure 'parse_status' for .xlsx is no longer 'openpyxl_unavailable'.

planned_changes: Export a CSV (cbtc_braking_margin_cells.csv) containing the key computed cells for each train: speed_kmh, v_mps, latency_s, emergency_mps2, stopping_distance_m, separation_m, available_margin_m, classification, recommendation. Place the CSV in the artifacts directory and reference it in safety_case_addendum.md.
Take and attach at least one screenshot (PNG) of the spreadsheet showing the formula bar and the rows used for T1-T3; include an alternate PDF render if PNG is not possible.
Rerun the workbook parsing step in an environment with openpyxl available. Save and attach the parser run log (workbook_parsed_ok.txt) and update the run metadata to reflect successful parsing.

Update safety_case_addendum.md to include a short 'Numeric precision and rounding' section specifying displayed decimals, rounding mode (e.g., round half up), and the exact values used in the addendum (show to 4-6 decimal places).
Add a short justification paragraph for classification thresholds (35 m and 45 m) explaining the operational or safety basis or citing the relevant policy/document used to choose them.
Explicitly document why service_brake_mps2 is present but not used in the stopping-distance formula, and include it as a validation column in CSV for traceability.

Add a reproducible verification script (recompute_margins.py) that reads input/task.json, recomputes stopping_distance and available_margin using the stated formulas, and asserts that differences versus workbook outputs are within a defined tolerance (e.g., 0.5 m). Attach the script and a short run log.
Add a one-page QA checklist (qa_checks.txt) stating that: CSV present, screenshot attached, parser log attached, rounding policy stated, thresholds justified, and recompute script run successfully.
